subject: Mathematics

unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 15! In this unit you will become a confident handler of data – the information we collect, organise, display and interpret to understand the world. Data is all around you. When Amina records the daily temperature in Mogadishu, when Yusuf counts how many customers visit the family shop each hour, or when Halima surveys her class about their favourite fruit, they are all working with data. Learning to make sense of that information is one of the most useful skills in all of mathematics.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, activities and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured from the Cambridge Primary Mathematics 0096 — Stage 6 workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: This unit is designed for self-paced learning. That means you move at your own speed. Read each explanation slowly, copy every worked example onto paper and try it yourself, and only move on when you feel ready. Your AI tutor is available day and night to give you extra practice, explain a step in a new way, generate fresh data sets, or check your working. Your teacher will also meet with you about twice a week to help with anything that feels tricky.

Why does data matter so much? We live in a world flooded with information. Traders study sales data to decide what to stock. Doctors track patient data to spot outbreaks of illness. Farmers near Hargeisa record rainfall to plan their planting. Governments count populations to build the right number of schools and wells. People who understand data can ask good questions, spot patterns, make predictions, and avoid being tricked by dishonest claims. People who cannot, must simply trust whatever they are told. This unit puts you firmly in the first group.

As you work, keep a pencil, a ruler and some squared paper beside you. Data handling is a doing subject – you must draw the charts, count the tallies, and calculate the averages yourself. If you make a mistake, that is not a failure; it is a signpost showing you exactly what to practise next. Every scientist, engineer and analyst you admire once learned these very same ideas, starting exactly where you are now.
Tell the difference between categorical and numerical data, and between discrete and continuous data.
Design a fair survey question and collect data using a tally chart.
Organise raw data into a frequency table.
Draw and interpret bar charts and pictograms for categorical data.
Draw and interpret line graphs for data that changes over time.
Calculate the sector angles for and interpret a pie chart.
Find the mean, median, mode and range of a set of numbers.

concepts.title: Types of Data
concepts.explanation: Before you can choose how to collect or display information, you must know what type of data you are dealing with. Getting this right is the first decision every data handler makes, because the type of data decides which charts and which averages make sense. Categorical data sorts things into groups or categories. The answers are usually words, not amounts. Favourite sport (football, basketball, swimming), type of pet (goat, cat, chicken), and mode of travel to the masjid (walking, car, bus) are all categorical.…
concepts.example: Numerical data is counted or measured with numbers. The number of camels a family owns, the heights of students in centimetres, and the daily temperature are all numerical. Numerical data comes in two kinds. Discrete…

concepts.title: Collecting Data and Frequency Tables
concepts.explanation: Good analysis depends on good data. If your data is collected carelessly, no clever chart can rescue it. The most common way to collect data is a survey – asking people a clear question and recording their answers. The way you word the question matters enormously. A good survey question is clear and unbiased. A biased question pushes people towards a particular answer. ‘Don’t you agree that football is the best sport?’ is a leading question – it nudges people to say yes. A fair version simply asks, ‘Which sport do…
concepts.example: As answers come in, the neatest way to count them is a tally chart. Each time an answer appears you make a single stroke, and the fifth stroke is drawn across the previous four to make a bundle of five. Counting in…

concepts.title: Bar Charts and Pictograms
concepts.explanation: Bar charts are the most common way to display categorical data. They use rectangular bars of equal width, and the height of each bar shows the frequency of that category. Because the taller bar is easy to spot, bar charts make comparison quick: you can see at a glance which category is most and least popular. Every good bar chart has the same parts, and you lose marks if any are missing:
concepts.example: A clear title saying what the chart shows.

concepts.title: Line Graphs for Change Over Time
concepts.explanation: Line graphs are the best choice when data changes over time. The horizontal axis almost always shows time – hours, days, months or years – and the vertical axis shows the quantity being measured. Each measurement is plotted as a point, and the points are joined with straight lines. The rising and falling line lets you see trends that would be hidden in a table of numbers. Suppose Ibrahim records the temperature in Hargeisa at midday every day for a week: Monday 18°C, Tuesday 20°C, Wednesday 22°C, Thursday 25°C,…
concepts.example: To read a line graph, look first for the overall trend: is the line generally going up (increasing), going down (decreasing), or staying level? Then find the highest and lowest points, and notice any sudden jumps. You…

concepts.title: Pie Charts and Part-to-Whole
concepts.explanation: A pie chart is a circle divided into sectors, where each sector shows a category’s share of the whole. Pie charts are excellent when you want to show how something is divided up – how a family budget is spent, or what fraction of students chose each option. Because the whole circle represents the total, pie charts answer the question ‘what part of the total is each group?’ better than any other chart. The key fact is that a full circle contains 360 degrees, and this 360° represents everyone in your data. To find…
concepts.example: ⭐ Pie Chart Angle Formula Angle for a category = (frequency for that category ÷ total frequency) × 360 degrees. All the sector angles must add up to 360 degrees.

concepts.title: Averages – Mean, Median and Mode
concepts.explanation: An average is a single value that stands in for a whole set of numbers – a way of describing what is ‘typical’. There are three different averages, and a big part of Year 6 is knowing not just how to calculate each one, but which to use and why. The mode is the value that appears most often. To find it, look for the value that is repeated the most. In the data 3, 4, 4, 4, 6, 9 the mode is 4. The mode is the only average that works for categorical data – it is how we say ‘mango was the most popular fruit’. A set…
concepts.example: The median is the middle value once the numbers are placed in order. First sort the data from smallest to largest, then find the middle. For the ordered set 78, 79, 85, 86, 88 the median is 85, the value in the centre.…

methods.title: How to find the median
methods.example: Classify each as categorical (C), discrete (D) or continuous (Con): (a) number of siblings, (b) favourite sport, (c) height in cm, (d) a bus route number.
steps: Write all the values in order, smallest to largest.
If there is an odd number of values, the median is the middle one.
If there is an even number, add the two middle values and divide by two.

methods.title: How to find the mean
methods.example: The number of books read in a month by 10 students: 3, 5, 2, 3, 4, 3, 2, 5, 4, 3. Make a frequency table and give the total.
steps: Add up every value to get the total.
Count how many values there are.
Divide the total by that count.

methods.title: How to draw a pie chart
methods.example: In a survey of 20 students about favourite lunch: Pizza 8, Rice 6, Sambusa 4, Salad 2. Find each sector angle.
steps: Find the total frequency.
For each category work out (frequency ÷ total) × 360°.
Check the angles add up to 360°.
Draw the circle, measure each sector with a protractor, then add a key and title.

methods.title: Mean, median and mode
methods.example: Find the mean, median and mode of these scores: 85, 90, 78, 85, 92, 88, 85, 79, 91, 86.
steps: Solution: Mode – the value 85 appears three times, more than any other, so the mode is 85.
There are 10 values, so the two middle ones are the 5th and 6th: 85 and 86.
The median is (85 + 86) ÷ 2 = 85.5.

methods.title: The range
methods.example: Find the range of these daily temperatures (°C): 22, 24, 23, 25, 21, 23, 24.
steps: Solution: The highest value is 25 and the lowest is 21.
The small range tells us the temperature was fairly steady all week.

methods.title: Compare two data sets
methods.example: Class A scores 75, 80, 85, 90, 95. Class B scores 70, 75, 85, 95, 100. Which class did better, and which was more consistent?
steps: Solution: Both classes have the same mean: Class A totals 425 ÷ 5 = 85, and Class B totals 425 ÷ 5 = 85.
So on average they performed equally.
Now the ranges: Class A is 95 − 75 = 20; Class B is 100 − 70 = 30.
Class A has the smaller range, so Class A was more consistent, even though the averages are equal.

workedExamples.title: Classify the data
workedExamples.prompt: Classify each as categorical (C), discrete (D) or continuous (Con): (a) number of siblings, (b) favourite sport, (c) height in cm, (d) a bus route number.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: (a) A whole-number count, so discrete. (b) Sorted into groups, so categorical. (c) A measurement that can take any value, so continuous. (d) A number used only as a label – you cannot average bus routes – so categorical. The test each time is: can I sensibly add or measure it?

workedExamples.title: Build a frequency table
workedExamples.prompt: The number of books read in a month by 10 students: 3, 5, 2, 3, 4, 3, 2, 5, 4, 3. Make a frequency table and give the total.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Count how many times each value appears. 2 appears twice, 3 appears four times, 4 appears twice, 5 appears twice. So the frequencies are: 2 → 2, 3 → 4, 4 → 2, 5 → 2. Check the total: 2 + 4 + 2 + 2 = 10, which matches the 10 students.

workedExamples.title: Find the pie chart angles
workedExamples.prompt: In a survey of 20 students about favourite lunch: Pizza 8, Rice 6, Sambusa 4, Salad 2. Find each sector angle.

workedExamples.title: Mean, median and mode
workedExamples.prompt: Find the mean, median and mode of these scores: 85, 90, 78, 85, 92, 88, 85, 79, 91, 86.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Mode – the value 85 appears three times, more than any other, so the mode is 85. Median – put them in order: 78, 79, 85, 85, 85, 86, 88, 90, 91, 92. There are 10 values, so the two middle ones are the 5th and 6th: 85 and 86. The median is (85 + 86) ÷ 2 = 85.5. Mean – the total is 859; divide by 10 to get 85.9.

workedExamples.title: The range
workedExamples.prompt: Find the range of these daily temperatures (°C): 22, 24, 23, 25, 21, 23, 24.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: The highest value is 25 and the lowest is 21. Range = 25 − 21 = 4°C. The small range tells us the temperature was fairly steady all week.

workedExamples.title: Compare two data sets
workedExamples.prompt: Class A scores 75, 80, 85, 90, 95. Class B scores 70, 75, 85, 95, 100. Which class did better, and which was more consistent?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Both classes have the same mean: Class A totals 425 ÷ 5 = 85, and Class B totals 425 ÷ 5 = 85. So on average they performed equally. Now the ranges: Class A is 95 − 75 = 20; Class B is 100 − 70 = 30. Class A has the smaller range, so Class A was more consistent, even though the averages are equal.

workedExamples.title: Read a pie chart
workedExamples.prompt: A family’s monthly budget pie chart shows Housing 40%, Food 25%, Transport 15%, Savings 10%, Other 10%. Their income is 4000 shillings. How much goes to housing, and how much more than transport?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Housing is 40% of 4000 = 0.40 × 4000 = 1600 shillings. Transport is 15% of 4000 = 0.15 × 4000 = 600 shillings. Housing minus transport = 1600 − 600 = 1000 shillings more on housing.

workedExamples.title: Spot the misleading graph
workedExamples.prompt: Two bar charts show the same data: Team A 85 points, Team B 82 points. Chart X has a scale from 0 to 100; Chart Y has a scale from 80 to 90. Which makes the gap look bigger, what is the true difference, and which is more honest?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Chart Y makes the gap look bigger, because its stretched scale starting at 80 exaggerates a small difference. The true difference is 85 − 82 = 3 points. Chart X, whose scale starts at 0, is more honest. Someone might use Chart Y to make a small lead look impressive.

workedExamples.title: Practice 9
workedExamples.prompt: A line graph of temperatures shows Mon 19, Tue 22, Wed 21, Thu 26, Fri 24 (°C). On which day was it hottest, and what was the range across the week?

workedExamples.title: Practice 10
workedExamples.prompt: In a pictogram, each ☺ symbol represents 4 students. How many students do 6 and a half symbols represent?

workedExamples.title: Practice 11
workedExamples.prompt: Find the mean, median and mode of: 3, 5, 3, 8, 6, 3, 7.

workedExamples.title: Practice 12
workedExamples.prompt: A set of six numbers has a mean of 15. Five of them are 10, 18, 12, 20 and 16. Find the sixth number.
workedExamples.solution: 1) The six numbers total 6 × 15 = 90; the five given total 76; the sixth number is 90 − 76 = 14.

activities.title: Family Survey and Bar Chart
activities.materials: paper, a ruler, a pencil, and some people to ask.
steps: Choose a clear, fair question, such as ‘What is your favourite fruit?’ Offer four choices.
Ask at least ten family members or neighbours and record each answer with a tally.
Turn your tallies into a frequency table and check the total matches how many people you asked.
Draw a neat bar chart with a title, labelled axes and an even scale. Which fruit won?
Keep your frequency table – you will reuse it in Activity 3 to build a pie chart.

activities.title: One Week of Temperatures (Line Graph)
activities.materials: paper, a pencil, and a way to check the daily temperature for a week.
steps: Each day at the same time, record the temperature (from a thermometer, phone or radio).
After seven days, draw a line graph with days along the bottom and temperature up the side.
Plot each point carefully and join them with straight lines.
Describe the trend: which day was hottest, which coldest, and did the week warm up or cool down?

activities.title: Turn Your Survey into a Pie Chart
activities.materials: your frequency table from Activity 1, a protractor, a compass or round object, and coloured pencils.
steps: For each category, calculate its angle: (frequency ÷ total) × 360°.
Add all your angles together and check they reach 360° before you draw.
Draw a circle, then measure each sector with your protractor from the centre.
Colour each sector and add a key and a title. Compare it with your bar chart – which shows the winner more clearly?

activities.title: The Averages Dice Game
activities.materials: a dice (or numbers 1–6 on folded paper), and a partner.
steps: Each player rolls the dice five times and writes down the five numbers.
Both players calculate the mean, median, mode and range of their own five rolls.
Check each other’s working. The player with the higher mean wins the round.
Play five rounds. Talk about how one big roll can pull the mean up but not always the median.

activities.title: Data Detective (Investigation)
activities.materials: a newspaper, a magazine, or a chart your family has seen online.
steps: Find any chart or graph and study it carefully.
Ask the ‘detective questions’: Where does the axis start? Are the labels clear? Is anything missing?
Decide whether the chart is honest or misleading, and explain why.
Redraw it more fairly if you think it is misleading. Share what you found with your teacher.

activities.title: Household Data Collection (Family Task)
steps: As a family, choose something worth measuring for a week: cups of water drunk, minutes spent reading, or how each person travels to work or school.
Everyone records their own data honestly each day.
At the end of the week, combine the data into a frequency table and choose the best chart to show it.
Discuss one conclusion your family can draw, and one decision it might help you make.

After your activities, talk or write about: Which activity helped you understand data best? Which chart was hardest to draw accurately, and why? What surprised you in the data you collected? What would you like more practice with? Share your answers with your teacher at your next session.

rules.title: Key idea 1
rules.text: So which average should you use? It depends on the data. Use the mode for categorical data, or when you want the most common choice. Use the mean when the data is fairly evenly spread and you want to take every value into account. Use the median when there are outliers – unusually high or low values that would drag the mean away from what is typical. Imagine a small neighbourhood where most families earn about 400 shillings a week but one trader earns 5000. The mean is pulled far above almost everyone, so the median describes the ‘typical’ family far more fairly. Choosing the right average is a genuine mathematical judgement, not a fixed rule.

terms: Data counted or measured with numbers
Whole counts / any value in a range
The overall direction of a line graph
A wedge-shaped slice of a pie chart
commonMistakes: Always put the data in order first
Dividing by the wrong number for the mean
Divide by how many values there are
Multiply symbols by the key value
Check where the vertical axis starts

realProblems.prompt: At the Eastleigh market, Sumaya records the number of customers each hour for 6 hours: 15, 22, 18, 25, 20, 20. Find the mean, median, mode and range.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

realProblems.prompt: A family divides its 4000-shilling weekly budget as a pie chart: Food 45%, Rent 25%, Transport 15%, Charity 10%, Savings 5%. How much goes to food, and how much to charity?
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

realProblems.prompt: Liban measures rainfall (mm) over a week: 0, 4, 6, 0, 10, 8, 14. Which chart best shows how it changed, what was the total, and on how many days did it rain?
realProblems.answer: Food 45% of 4000 = 1800 shillings; charity 10% of 4000 = 400 shillings.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Food 45% of 4000 = 1800 shillings; charity 10% of 4000 = 400 shillings.

realProblems.prompt: Two goalkeepers face the same number of shots each match. Over 5 matches, Hassan saves 8, 9, 8, 10, 9 and Abdi saves 5, 14, 3, 12, 6. Compare their means and ranges, and say who is the more reliable keeper.
realProblems.answer: A line graph; total = 42 mm; it rained on 5 days.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: A line graph; total = 42 mm; it rained on 5 days.

realProblems.prompt: A newspaper shows a bar chart where the sales axis starts at 950 instead of 0, making a rise from 980 to 1000 look huge. Explain why this is misleading and what the true increase is.
realProblems.answer: Hassan mean = 44 ÷ 5 = 8.8, range = 10 − 8 = 2; Abdi mean = 40 ÷ 5 = 8, range = 14 − 3 = 11; Hassan is more reliable — a slightly higher mean and a far smaller range.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Hassan mean = 44 ÷ 5 = 8.8, range = 10 − 8 = 2; Abdi mean = 40 ÷ 5 = 8, range = 14 − 3 = 11; Hassan is more reliable — a slightly higher mean and a far smaller range.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: A set of six numbers has a mean of 15. Five of them are 10, 18, 12, 20 and 16. Find the sixth number.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 1) The six numbers total 6 × 15 = 90; the five given total 76; the sixth number is 90 − 76 = 14.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Two shops record daily sales for 5 days — Shop A: 40, 42, 41, 39, 43 and Shop B: 20, 60, 35, 55, 30. Find each shop’s mean and range, and say which shop is more consistent.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Shop A mean = 205 ÷ 5 = 41, range = 43 − 39 = 4; Shop B mean = 200 ÷ 5 = 40, range = 60 − 20 = 40; Shop A is far more consistent.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: A survey of 60 people about how they travel: Walk 24, Bus 18, Car 12, Bicycle 6. Calculate the four pie chart angles and confirm they total 360°.
Explain, with an example of your own, why the median can be a fairer average than the mean when a data set contains an outlier.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Answers vary; a good answer gives a set such as 2, 3, 4, 5, 100 where the single large outlier (100) drags the mean up to about 22.8 even though four of the five values are below 6, so the median of 4 describes the typical value far better.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain the key idea in Pie Charts and Part-to-Whole.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: A pie chart is a circle divided into sectors, where each sector shows a category’s share of the whole. Pie charts are excellent when you want to show how something is divided up – how a family budget is spent, or what fraction of students chose each option. Because the whole circle represents the total, pie charts answer the question ‘what part of the total is each group?’ better than any other chart. The key fact is that a full circle contains 360 degrees, and this 360° represents everyone in your data. To find…

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain the key idea in Averages – Mean, Median and Mode.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: An average is a single value that stands in for a whole set of numbers – a way of describing what is ‘typical’. There are three different averages, and a big part of Year 6 is knowing not just how to calculate each one, but which to use and why. The mode is the value that appears most often. To find it, look for the value that is repeated the most. In the data 3, 4, 4, 4, 6, 9 the mode is 4. The mode is the only average that works for categorical data – it is how we say ‘mango was the most popular fruit’. A set…

selfAssessment: I can this unit is designed for self-paced learning. That means you move at your own speed. Read each explanation slowly, copy every worked example onto paper and try it yourself, and only move on when you feel ready. Your AI tutor is available day and night to give you extra practice, explain a step in a new way, generate fresh data sets, or check your working. Your teacher will also meet with you about twice a week to help with anything that feels tricky.

I can why does data matter so much? We live in a world flooded with information. Traders study sales data to decide what to stock. Doctors track patient data to spot outbreaks of illness. Farmers near Hargeisa record rainfall to plan their planting. Governments count populations to build the right number of schools and wells. People who understand data can ask good questions, spot patterns, make predictions, and avoid being tricked by dishonest claims. People who cannot, must simply trust whatever they are told. This unit puts you firmly in the first group.

I can as you work, keep a pencil, a ruler and some squared paper beside you. Data handling is a doing subject – you must draw the charts, count the tallies, and calculate the averages yourself. If you make a mistake, that is not a failure; it is a signpost showing you exactly what to practise next. Every scientist, engineer and analyst you admire once learned these very same ideas, starting exactly where you are now.
I can tell the difference between categorical and numerical data, and between discrete and continuous data.
I can design a fair survey question and collect data using a tally chart.
I can organise raw data into a frequency table.
I can draw and interpret bar charts and pictograms for categorical data.
I can draw and interpret line graphs for data that changes over time.
